title: Russia-Ukraine Ceasefire Negotiations
description: Tracking the evolving ceasefire diplomacy in the Russia-Ukraine war — from Trump-Putin phone calls to competing unilateral truces, Victory Day dynamics, and the role of US mediation in pushing toward a durable peace or temporary pause.

timeline.title: Trump-Putin 90-Minute Phone Call — Ceasefire Floated
timeline.summary: Trump told reporters he suggested 'a little bit of a ceasefire' to Putin, believing he might go along. Putin's adviser Yuri Ushakov confirmed the call lasted about 90 minutes. Putin indicated he was ready to declare a temporary ceasefire in honor of Victory Day (May 9).

timeline.title: Zelenskyy Seeks Details of Putin's May 9 Ceasefire Proposal
timeline.summary: Ukrainian President Zelenskyy said he instructed his team to contact the Trump administration to clarify details of Russia's Victory Day ceasefire proposal. Kyiv's position: a temporary holiday truce is insufficient; a durable peace requires full territorial terms.

timeline.title: Russia and Ukraine Declare Competing Ceasefires
timeline.summary: Russia's Ministry of Defence declared a unilateral ceasefire from May 8–9 tied to Victory Day commemorations, simultaneously warning of a 'massive missile strike' if Ukraine violated it. Ukraine countered by declaring its own truce for May 5–6, calling Russia's proposal politically motivated and 'not serious.'

timeline.title: Russia's Victory Day Parade to Proceed Without Military Hardware
timeline.summary: For the first time in nearly two decades, Russia's Victory Day parade in Moscow will include no tanks, missiles, or military equipment — a direct response to Ukraine's long-range drone capability. A strike killed two people deep inside Russia days before the parade.

timeline.title: Ukraine's Unilateral Truce Window Closes; Eyes Turn to Russia's May 8–9 Pause
timeline.summary: Ukraine's self-declared May 5–6 ceasefire expired without major incident. Attention shifted to whether Russia would honor its own May 8–9 Victory Day ceasefire as Moscow simultaneously warned of 'massive missile strikes' if Kyiv broke the truce. Western analysts continued to characterize the rival truces as parallel political theater rather than an off-ramp.

timeline.title: Dueling Victory Day Ceasefires Collapse on Day One — 1,820 Violations Logged
timeline.summary: Defense News and Time reported the rival truces had 'collapsed almost immediately.' Russia launched 108 combat drones and three missiles overnight May 5–6, striking Kharkiv among other cities. By 10 a.m. May 6, Zelenskyy's office logged 1,820 ceasefire violations — including ~30 assault operations and 20+ airstrikes using over 70 guided glide bombs. Zelenskyy accused Putin of 'utter cynicism' after Russia hit Ukrainian energy infrastructure during the supposed truce window.

timeline.title: Victory Day: Russia Holds Most Scaled-Back Parade in Putin Era — No Tanks, No Missiles, Only 2 Foreign Leaders
timeline.summary: Russia's May 9 Victory Day parade was the most stripped-down in decades: no heavy weapons, only infantry, with just Laos and Malaysia sending heads of state. Zelensky formally excluded Red Square from Ukraine's weapons plan for the one-hour ceremony but warned that war resumes immediately after. Ukraine struck a Russian Karakurt-class missile ship at Kaspiysk naval base ahead of the parade. 208 combat engagements recorded in the past day; Russian unilateral ceasefire nominally in force but broadly violated.

timeline.title: Trump Brokers 3-Day Ceasefire (May 9–11) With 1,000-Prisoner Swap — Day 1 Sees 140+ Violations
timeline.summary: President Trump announced a 3-day ceasefire between Russia and Ukraine tied to WWII Victory Day, including an exchange of 1,000 prisoners per side. Both Zelenskyy and Putin's adviser Ushakov confirmed the agreement. Day 1 saw Ukraine log 140+ Russian attacks, 10 assaults, and 850+ drone sorties; Moscow said it responded in kind to Kyiv's violations. Trump called the truce the 'beginning of the end of a very long, deadly, and hard fought War.'

timeline.title: Ceasefire Day 3 (Final) — 200+ Clashes Logged Weekend, Kremlin Caps Truce at 3 Days, No Extension Announced
timeline.summary: The final day of Trump's May 9–11 truce arrived with Reuters reporting more than 200 battlefield clashes since Saturday and three civilians killed in Russian drone strikes near the frontline. Ukraine declined to formally declare Russia in violation; Moscow said it 'responded in kind' to dozens of Ukrainian drones. The Kremlin explicitly stated the ceasefire is 'for three days, not longer,' pre-empting extension speculation. The 1,000-prisoner swap is reported to have proceeded. Trump said 'talks are continuing' and that the truce may be 'the beginning of the end.' Whether any framework survives midnight is the open question.

timeline.title: Ceasefire Expires — War Resumes, No Extension, Putin Signals Conditional Openness to Talks
timeline.summary: Trump's 3-day May 9–11 ceasefire lapsed with combat resuming on both sides. Ukraine logged 200+ Russian violations during the truce period; three civilians were killed in Russian drone strikes near the frontline. The Kremlin explicitly capped the truce at three days and ruled out extension. The 1,000-prisoner swap proceeded as agreed. Putin told Al Jazeera he remains willing to meet Zelenskyy in a neutral third country if a peace framework is finalized — but no framework is on the table. Both sides returned to full operational tempo as of May 12.

timeline.title: Post-Ceasefire Tally Wars: Moscow Claims 16,071 Ukrainian Violations; Kyiv Reports Two Civilians Killed in Kharkiv/Kherson
timeline.summary: Russia's Defense Ministry claimed Ukraine violated the May 9–11 ceasefire 16,071 times and said Russia 'responded symmetrically'; Ukrainian authorities reported Russian drones, bombs, and artillery struck civilian areas in Kharkiv and Kherson, killing at least two and wounding seven. The 1,000-each prisoner exchange reportedly completed. No new diplomatic framework has emerged; Trump's attention has fully rotated to Beijing and the Iran file. Polymarket odds of a Russia-Ukraine ceasefire by June 30, 2026 dropped sharply post-expiry.

timeline.title: Largest Russian Strike on Kyiv of Entire War — 670+ Drones, 56 Missiles, 12+ Killed — Hours After Putin Said War Was 'Coming to an End'
timeline.summary: Overnight into May 15, Russia executed what Kyiv mayor Vitali Klitschko called 'the enemy's largest-scale attack on the capital' since the February 2022 invasion. At least 12 people killed and 57+ injured in Kyiv. Strikes also hit Poltava and Zaporizhzhia, damaging energy and rail infrastructure. Across the 24-hour window Ukraine's air force tracked 670+ Shahed-type attack drones and 56 missiles, part of a broader strike of 1,560+ drones. Kyiv declared a day of mourning. The escalation comes days after Putin publicly suggested the war is 'coming to an end' and that he is open to a third-country meeting with Zelenskyy if a deal is finalized — Ukrainian analysts read the strikes as coercive bargaining to weaken Kyiv's starting position before the next negotiation rather than de-escalation. Russian cumulative combat losses since February 2022 reach approximately 1,345,240 per Ukrainian figures.

timeline.title: Kyiv Apartment-Block Death Toll Reaches 24; Zelensky Orders Retaliatory Strike Plan on Russian Oil and Weapons Industry
timeline.summary: Friday update lifts the death toll from Thursday's Russian missile strike on a Kyiv apartment block to 24, including three children — one a 15-year-old girl. Zelensky laid red roses at the rubble and met with senior military and intelligence officials to plan retaliatory long-range strikes 'using all necessary resources.' He framed the targets explicitly: Russia's oil industry, weapons industry, and those directly responsible for committing war crimes. Air-force data for the May 13–14 strike package: 94% of the 1,567 drones intercepted but only 7% of the 56 missiles. Zelensky called on Western allies to make sanctions 'more painful' and harder to circumvent — 'such a Russia can never be normalized.' The 1,000-per-side prisoner exchange announced under the lapsed May 9–11 Trump ceasefire reportedly continued during the week.

timeline.title: Ukrainian Drones Set Rosneft's Ryazan Refinery Ablaze — 'Oil Rain' Over the City; Trump-Zelensky Said to Be at 90–95% Agreement on Written Peace Proposal
timeline.summary: Direct fulfilment of Zelensky's Friday retaliation order arrived overnight on May 15 and dominated weekend coverage: Ukrainian long-range drones struck Rosneft's Ryazan oil refinery — one of Russia's largest, processing up to 17.1M tonnes/year of crude, ~460km from the Ukrainian border. Vacuum-distillation units ignited; Russian governor Pavel Malkov reported 3 killed and 12 injured (Ukrainian sources say 4 killed); schools and kindergartens ordered closed; locals shared photos of sticky black droplets covering cars and apartment windows ('oil rain'). Regional state of emergency declared. A coordinated strike hit a Rostov-on-Don warehouse said to hold ~6,000 FPV drones in three containers. On the diplomatic track, Reuters and Bloomberg reporting on Saturday-Sunday says Trump and Zelensky now agree on 90–95% of a written peace proposal, with Russian sign-off the remaining gap. The 1,000-per-side prisoner exchange announced under the lapsed May 9–11 Trump ceasefire reportedly continued during the week. Combat tempo unchanged: 263 engagements over the past day; Russian losses since February 2022 ~1,347,620 per Ukrainian figures.

timeline.title: Ukraine's Largest Single-Night Drone Strike on Russia Since the 2022 Invasion — 500+ UAVs, 556 Reported Intercepted by Russian MoD, Debris on Vnukovo Airport, 4 Killed Around Moscow; Trump-Zelensky Written Proposal Still at 90–95%
timeline.summary: Overnight from Saturday into Sunday May 17, Ukraine flew what TASS pegged at 500+ drones and what Russia's Ministry of Defence said it intercepted 556 of, with a further 30 shot down later on Sunday — described by Russian state media itself as the largest single-night Ukrainian drone attack on the Moscow capital region since the February 2022 invasion. Local authorities reported at least four killed (three near Moscow) and around a dozen wounded; debris from drones intercepted over the Moscow oblast (81 alone) fell on Vnukovo, Russia's largest airport, without causing structural damage. Russian air defences also intercepted drones over Bryansk, Belgorod, Kaluga and Tula. President Zelensky publicly confirmed the strikes, calling them 'entirely justified' — explicitly framed as the political-leverage follow-on to Saturday's economic-leverage strike at Rosneft's Ryazan refinery, both in fulfilment of Friday's retaliation order after the 24-dead Kyiv apartment-block strike. Russia counter-launched 287 drones into Ukraine overnight; the Ukrainian Air Force shot down all but eight across seven locations, with at least nine injured in Dnipropetrovsk and Zaporizhzhia. The Reuters/Bloomberg read on the written peace text remains 90–95% agreed between Trump and Zelensky with Russian sign-off the outstanding gap; analysts split on whether the Moscow-airspace operation strengthens or weakens Kyiv's seat at that table.
